context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ugh this reflection is scrambling my brain tbh, i kinda don’t get what the prompt is even asking. can u just explain what it wants in simple words?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it's a dense prompt! But before I break it down for you, let's try something: read the prompt one more time and tell me how many distinct questions you think it's actually asking. Just list them out in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and asking for a plain-language breakdown of the prompt. I should acknowledge their frustration, but rather than paraphrasing the entire prompt for them (which would do the work for them), I should use a Socratic approach to help them break it down themselves. I'll start by asking them to identify what they think the prompt's key questions are, which will get them engaged with the material without me spoon-feeding them.

exchanges.student: idk maybe like 2? one abt law causing development, and one abt institutions i guess, but i’m not really sure what counts as separate rn.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — you're on the right track with those two areas. But look more closely at the prompt: do you think the question about law and development is actually just one question, or are there two slightly different things being asked there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a reasonable attempt — they identified two broad areas. I should affirm this partial progress, but push them to look more carefully. The prompt actually contains three distinct questions: (1) what is the basis for the belief that law leads to development, (2) is law a tool for development or a goal in itself, and (3) what are institutions, can they be unbundled, and what is law's role as an institution compared to others. I'll nudge them to re-read more carefully without giving this breakdown away.

exchanges.student: wait maybe yeah it’s 2 there? like “why do ppl think law helps development” vs “is law just a way to get development or part of development itself,” but ngl they sound basically the same to me.
exchanges.tutor: They do sound similar, but think about it this way: is money a tool you use to get things you want, or is being wealthy itself the goal? How might that same distinction apply to law and development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified the two sub-questions about law, which is good progress. They're struggling to see the distinction between them, which is actually a meaningful conceptual difference (instrumental vs. intrinsic view of law). Rather than explaining the difference, I should ask them a question that helps them discover it themselves. I'll use a concrete analogy-style question to help them think about the difference between a means and an end.

exchanges.student: ok i kinda see it, but then i get lost again bc “law” feels super broad fr. like do they mean courts, property rights, constitutions, contracts, all of it?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a fair instinct — and the prompt itself gestures at this. Look at the third question again: it asks about 'unbundling' institutions. What do you think that word 'unbundle' might mean in this context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting distracted by trying to pin down a precise definition of 'law' before engaging with the conceptual questions. This is a bit of a detour — the prompt isn't asking them to define law exhaustively, but rather to think about its relationship to development and institutions. I should acknowledge this is a fair observation but redirect them toward the third question in the prompt, which actually addresses this: the idea of institutions and whether they can be 'unbundled.' That's where the prompt itself invites them to think about what law is relative to other things.

exchanges.student: maybe like breaking institutions into parts? but then idk what the parts even are, and i’m still not sure how law is diff from institutions overall.
exchanges.tutor: Good intuition on unbundling. Now think about this: can you name two or three things other than law that might also count as 'institutions' in a society? What do they all have in common?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuition about 'unbundling' but is now conflating law and institutions, which is a key conceptual distinction the prompt wants them to grapple with. Rather than explaining the difference, I should ask them a simple question that helps them see law as one type of institution among others, nudging them toward thinking about what other institutions exist alongside law.

exchanges.student: umm maybe markets, bureaucracy, family/community norms? i honestly don’t know what they have in common besides “they affect how ppl act” lol.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really solid working definition of institutions — don't undersell it! So now, given that, what do you think makes law *specific* or *distinct* as an institution compared to, say, family norms or markets?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given excellent examples and actually landed on a solid working definition of institutions ('they affect how people act') without realizing it. I should affirm this and push them to now place law within that framework — what makes law a specific kind of institution compared to the others they listed? This moves them toward the core of the third question.

exchanges.student: maybe bc law is official and enforced by the state? but then i’m stuck on what i’m supposed to do with that in the paper tbh.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong observation — hold onto it. Now, you've identified three questions the prompt is asking. Do you see a logical order or connection between them that could guide how you structure your reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good conceptual point about law being state-backed and formally enforced. Now they're asking how to use all this in the paper. This is a good moment to zoom out and connect the three questions they've identified back to the assignment. Rather than telling them how to structure it, I should ask them to think about how these three questions might relate to each other as a coherent argument — this pushes them toward synthesis without doing the writing for them.

exchanges.student: not really tbh, it still feels like 3 random mini-essays smashed together. i think i need a super simple map or i’m gonna ramble.
exchanges.tutor: I get the frustration, but I won't map it out for you — that's your job as the writer. Here's a nudge though: which of the three questions do you think you'd need to answer *first* before the others even make sense?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and feeling lost about structure. However, giving them a map would be doing the work for them. Instead, I should push them to find the logical thread themselves by asking a leading question about which of the three questions feels most foundational — i.e., which one do the others depend on? This helps them discover the structure organically.

exchanges.student: maybe the institutions one first? or the law one first? idk, that’s exactly where my brain is blank rn.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: if you don't yet know what an institution *is*, can you meaningfully discuss law's role as one? Which question has to come first logically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is genuinely stuck on sequencing. They're going back and forth without progress. Rather than letting them spiral, I should ask a more targeted question: if you don't know what an institution is, can you meaningfully ask whether law is one? This should help them see that the institutions question is definitionally prior, without giving them the structure outright.

exchanges.student: ok yeah then institutions first i guess, but i still need help seeing the whole flow bc rn it just feels muddy.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built up a lot here — you defined institutions, placed law within them, and found your starting point. At this stage, talking more won't clear the mud as much as trying to write will. Can you draft just a 2-3 sentence opening that sets up your reflection, starting with institutions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified that the institutions question comes first logically. They've made real progress across this conversation: they identified the three questions, defined institutions reasonably well, noted law's distinctiveness as state-backed, and found a starting point for structure. We're at exchange 9 and they keep asking for more scaffolding rather than attempting to write. Per the rules about redundancy and integrating discussion into writing, I should now push them to attempt a written outline or opening paragraph rather than continuing to talk abstractly. I should not give them the flow.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. No direct answers were given; instead, the tutor consistently used targeted questions to guide the student toward their own understanding. The student was led to identify the prompt's sub-questions, define institutions independently, distinguish law from other institutions, and determine a logical essay structure — all through their own reasoning. Responses were concise, well-paced, and supportive without being evaluative in a grading sense.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under 1.1 because the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently built on each student response (e.g., affirming the working definition of institutions in Turn 6, holding onto the state-enforcement insight in Turn 7) and used diagnostic questioning before advancing. Under 1.3, the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'that's your job as the writer' in Turn 8, redirecting to logical ordering) rather than vague praise. Dialogue quality was strong: the conversation progressed without repetitive cycling, stayed anchored to the assignment, and the tutor redirected the student's request for a 'map' without engaging off-task. Communication was bite-sized and interactive throughout, and tone was warm and encouraging without implying formal grading.
